posts.name: John Pollard
posts.cooked: So word on the street is Louis Latour’s 2016 Grand Cru RSV is next level good. Anyone know if it was a lucky vintage for them or if they’re making solid improvements?

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: had to be luck if they still bake the reds.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked: Yup, still copper kettles for all, as far as I am aware.

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked: Can you elaborate Rob? Hard to believe that producers of Burgundy in this day and age are careless about production. They do know these wines are quite valuable no?

posts.name: Michael Bowden
posts.cooked:
Dennis Borczon:

Can you elaborate Rob? Hard to believe that producers of Burgundy in this day and age are careless about production. They do know these wines are quite valuable no?

They flash pasteurize the wines which many people believe strips the wine of flavor and hurts the overall quality of the final product.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: From barrel it was quite good, though I don’t think any better than the 2015. Next level would be going too far, but it does seem that the reds here are improving.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
As mentioned above by Mr Bowden, they use copper kettles to heat the juice, flash pasteurizing it, for all reds.
Seems like Allan Meadows chimed in a few days ago with his Côte de Beaune '16 reviews.
Gave the RSV a score in the high eighties-low nineties.
Next level.
:0

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked: Can any wine be good when you flash pasteurize it? Are they that afraid of microbial infection?

As far as I understand it, thermovinification has a variety of effects, foremost enhancing extraction of certain compounds, including polyphenols. It also helps control microbial spoilage and may have stabilizing effects. It is more popular than you might think and whether it is detrimental to making great wine seems like a complicated issue. Beaucastel has used it for decades.

Wines Vines Analytics

Thermovinification Heats Up Interest

Heat is the new hot topic in winemaking. After decades of relative obscurity, interest in thermovinification—heating grapes and must to near-boiling temperatures prior to fermentation—is simmering nicely in California wine circles. Fans of...

Interesting article, thanks for sharing. I purchased a case with the intention to cellar for the long-haul. Do you think this primer technique affects long-term aging?

posts.cooked: Does anyone know when they started flashing at Latour?

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
Found this article, from 1983…

nytimes.com – 4 Sep 83

WINE; THE BURGANDY QUESTION (Published 1983)

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
I love how the author presents it as astounding that the 1983 “peasants” are using home computers to keep track of their orders…
Anybody wanna take a stab at the technological face of Burgundy 35 years from now?
Reverse engineered DRC?
The Hautes Cotes making Lodi style zinfandel?
Anti-hail cannons double as tourist launchers at the new Disney Beaune?
The Hospices de Beaune is back in business as a resurrection and cloning center?
Oh the possibilities…

Just curious, has anyone had Louis Latour Romanee-Saint-Vivant with significant age (25+ years)? Does it hold up well?

posts.name: Fred C
posts.cooked:
Tried a 99 a couple of years ago. I was hugely disappointed. Now it makes sense.

  • 1999 Louis Latour Romanée St. Vivant Les Quatre Journaux - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Romanée St. Vivant Grand Cru (3/16/2016)
    $175 for a great vintage RSV is a good deal right? Wrong.

On the nose quite promising with stems, bright red fruit. On the palate a mess. So much oak it was bitter. Dark. Nothing like a RSV

Followed this over 5 days and it never improved. Picked up some odd kirsch liquor note.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
JohnP:

Interesting article, thanks for sharing. I purchased a case with the intention to cellar for the long-haul. Do you think this primer technique affects long-term aging?



JohnP:

Thanks @RobertPanzer, that was a good read!
Just curious, has anyone had Louis Latour Romanee-Saint-Vivant with significant age (25+ years)? Does it hold up well?

I´ve had a fair share of L.Latour old/mature L.Latour wines, incl. RSV.
They certainly hold “well” - some of the older bottles (1964, 1970/71/72, 1976, 1978 …1985 … ) were really excellent …
However, they also do possess (imho) a certain typic hous-style which (most probably) comes from the pasteurisation, a kind of candy-sweet note, like artificial raspberries … not always in the foreground (more to be found in the simplier wines), but nevertheless quite often hints of it present … and these wines are sometimes a bit lacking the last bit of elegance … like to exspect from a RSV or Chambertin …
Just imho …

This is good feedback, thank you everyone. I was able to get 2016s for $500 a bottle. It was actually my first Grand-Cru purchase. What GC would you recommend (no more than $500 retail) that would deliver that elegance and complexity (20+ years later) in a good vintage such as 2016, over the LL RSV?

posts.name: Marcus Dean
posts.cooked: Any good producers Suchot or RSV will give you that elegance with sufficient age, Hudelot and Arnoux both make good examples of both vineyards. And if you like that refined elegant style look into Chambolle and Volnay as well

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
Ouch. Quite the first go with GC Burg purchase…6k on a blind purchase for your first go?
Who was the genius who told you it was “next level”?
I would exchange his genius hat for a dunce cap.
For starters, I would dial your threshold way down from $500, and taste different producers to find a house whose style you appreciate.

I still can’t believe that I see people actually quoting Suckling in sales offers.
Garagiste.
Woodland Hills.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked: Ok. So to actually contribute something on topic, I visited Latour in 2007, which is why the copper kettles are forever in mind when I think of the house. I thought it novel and charming at the time, in fact, a bit like visiting a living museum. There is some wicked serious confiture de cave growing on those walls. I didn’t have any judgement about the pasteurization practice. I have enjoyed many of their wines in my burgeoning burg days working retail, principally because there wasn’t a lot available, and Latour was. The Aloxe 1er Chaillots stands out in my memory as one of the more profound Aloxe 1er wines I had yet encoutered. A solid buy at the time.

pretty normal that merchants quote whoever gave the top score for a wine, most of the buying public dont have a clue who the critic is.

posts.name: John Pollard
posts.cooked:
Robert Panzer:

Ouch. Quite the first go with GC Burg purchase…6k on a blind purchase for your first go?
Who was the genius who told you it was “next level”?
I would exchange his genius hat for a dunce cap.
For starters, I would dial your threshold way down from $500, and taste different producers to find a house whose style you appreciate.

I only went for the 6-pack. Tasted the 2014, 2012 and 2010 RSV with Bernard from Louis Latour back in November at the San Diego Wine Festival. Thought it was tasty juice, but wanted to wait for a better vintage to jump in. Hopefully, with the limited supply (Bernard said only 35 cases of 2016 RSV will be sent to the US) and great vintage, my investment was sound. However, I do agree that it was a lofty effort. Had I not done the tasting in Nov, I probably would not be buying GC at all. Totally guilty for letting Suckling’s score weigh on my decision.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked: And that sounds like you are on the right track… Taste. Buy what you enjoy and can afford.

JohnP:

Thanks @RobertPanzer, that was a good read!
Just curious, has anyone had Louis Latour Romanee-Saint-Vivant with significant age (25+ years)? Does it hold up well?

Sadly my only real experience was a 1985 which had died when I tasted it around 2000. Just secured a couple of wines from the 1970s (not RSV) and will be opening a bottle next month.

posts.name: John Pollard
posts.cooked:
Mark Golodetz:

Sadly my only real experience was a 1985 which had died when I tasted it around 2000. Just secured a couple of wines from the 1970s (not RSV) and will be opening a bottle next month.

Mark, look forward to hear if 1970s LL hold up. Did you secure them on auction?

@ John P: I had a brilliant 1993 L. Latour Bonnes Mares about 5-6 years ago…it was lovely. That being said…I’ve never really cared for their wines accept for that one time. That being said, I’d strongly consider spending your money on a few other producers that cost far FAR less and you can have some amazing, true, “Grand Cru” experiences from producers that make affordable Corton, Clos Vougeot, or Echezeaux. If you’re looking to try the Vosne Romanee GC’s before you can’t afford them…I’d look for back vintages with some age on them that are around the same price and closer to being ready to enjoy. I wish you the best of luck with your purchase…I hope it pays off in 25+ years.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
I was neutral on the subject of Louis Latour red (have had some really good ones, some not so good) until the 1970 Corton Grancey at Bern’s a few months back. I am now actively looking for Latour reds with bottle age (and right price and provenance).

Dan Kravitz
